data.title: Chicago Fed’s Goolsbee: Inflation shocks from the energy markets crisis will delay interest rates in 2026
data.description:
Chicago Federal Reserve President Austan Goolsbee signaled that persistent inflation shocks from energy markets will likely delay anticipated interest rate cuts in 2026, warning that the Fed faces a critical credibility test as inflation has remained above target for five consecutive years.

“The longer this inflation disruption goes, the more likely it is, in my view, that the appropriate rate cutting would be put off,” Goolsbee said, adding that piling a new energy shock on top of existing tariff-driven inflation puts the central bank in “a dangerous slippery spot.”

Speaking at a panel discussion at the Semafor World Economy 2026 conference in Washington, DC, Goolsbee acknowledged the Fed’s earlier misjudgment on “transitory” inflation has left lasting scars.

“Your credibility took a hit. We need to be extremely careful,” he said, noting that 2025 saw progress on inflation stall out completely.

Goolsbee emphasized that the U.S. is better positioned to weather an extended period of high oil prices than it was during the 1970s oil shocks because the country has become a “massive energy producer.”

While consumers and transportation sectors will feel the “pinch,” he predicted investment in fracking would likely ramp back up, tempering the overall economic impact.

On questions about political pressure on the Fed, Goolsbee firmly defended the central bank’s independence, noting the Federal Reserve Act requires the FOMC to focus solely on price stability and maximum employment.

“It doesn’t say anything in the Federal Reserve Act about make the White House happy, make the stock market happy,” he said, adding that “there’s no bad weather. There’s only bad clothing.”

Consumer spending remains the backbone of U.S. economic growth, Goolsbee noted, though he flagged concern over record-low consumer sentiment driven by rising gasoline prices.

“If that catches a cold, if that sneezes, then the economy is going to catch a cold,” he warned, while observing that spending has not yet faltered despite the dramatic drop in confidence.

Turning to artificial intelligence, Goolsbee expressed skepticism that current productivity gains can be attributed to AI adoption, which he said “hasn’t been big enough” yet.

He cautioned that elevated stock valuations based on future AI benefits could trigger a “boom-bust cycle” similar to the dot-com era, warning that the Fed might need to raise rates to prevent overheating.

“Those issues of boom and bust and valuations and spending out of what’s going to be future bounty—we better pay attention,” he said.

More on the U.S. economy

  • Warsh hearing set for next week, Sen. Scott says - report
  • Gas price shock will only partly offset tax refunds - JPMorgan
  • Producer Price Index maintains pace in March but comes below consensus
  • Tuesday’s Economic Calendar
  • IEA head says oil prices will soon reflect severity of Iran crisis

data.title: JPMorgan rebuffs Trump's argument related to moving debanking case to New York - report
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase (JPM) said President Donald Trump's argument related to moving the $5B debanking lawsuit against the bank and CEO Jamie Dimon to New York "makes no sense", Bloomberg News reported.

In January, Trump is said to have filed a $5B lawsuit in the state court of Miami-Dade County, Florida, alleging the bank blacklisted him and his businesses for political reasons.

The suit alleged JPMorgan (JPM) committed trade libel and breached its implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, Fox Business had reported, citing the complaint, which was filed in Miami-Dade County state court. Trump also alleged that Dimon ran afoul of Florida's Unfair and Deceptive Trade Practices Act.

JPM moved the lawsuit to U.S. district court in Miami last month, and was seeking to move it to Manhattan federal court in New York, according to the Tuesday, April 14, report by Bloomberg.

Trump reportedly contends the allegation against Dimon means account agreements requiring disputes to be litigated in New York don't apply.

However, lawyers for JPM and Dimon reportedly said in a Miami federal court filing that the defendants are not aware of any blacklist and can only proceed based on plaintiffs' allegations.

Even if Trump had provided evidence to back his claim, the account agreement would still require the case to be heard in New York, the bank reportedly said.

Earlier, JPMorgan had stated that "the suit has no merit," as the bank "does not close accounts for political or religious reasons." Rather, accounts get closed when "they create legal or regulatory risk for the company," it said.

Separately, Trump's privately-owned conglomerate in March sued Capital One Financial (COF), accusing it of wrongfully terminating more than 300 company accounts in 2021.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: ACG Metals Limited reports FY25 results
data.description:
• ACG Metals Limited press release (ACGAF): FY revenue of $135.6M (+135.0% Y/Y)  misses by $105.4M.
  • Adjusted EBITDA of $76.3M generated for the year; reported net loss of $43M
  • Net assets declined sharply to $49.8M in FY2025 from $458.3M in FY2024.
  • The company ended FY2025 with net debt of $55M and a strong cash position of $145M, including $46M of restricted cash.

data.title: S&P 500 pops 1% as Trump hints of 'imminent' talks with Iran
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were comfortably in the green on Tuesday as investors grew optimistic about potential U.S.-Iran peace talks that could ease tensions in the Middle East.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was +1.1%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.7%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.6%.

The gains followed comments from President Donald Trump indicating that Iran had reached out for potential negotiations, even as the U.S. moved forward with a naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

Trump suggested that negotiations with Iran could take place “over the next two days” in Pakistan. Asian equities also rallied on hopes that a deal could lower oil prices and support global growth.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 2 basis points lower at 4.27%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 1 basis point to 3.77%.

On the economic front, the March U.S. Producer Price Index came in at +0.5% MoM vs. +1.2% consensus and +0.5% prior (revised from +0.7%), and +4.0% YoY vs. +4.7% consensus and +3.4% prior.

Core PPI, which excludes foods and energy, was +0.1% MoM vs. +0.5% consensus and +0.3% prior (revised from +0.5%), and +3.8% YoY vs. +4.2% consensus and +3.8% prior (revised from +3.9%).

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were lower at $92.3, while Brent (CO1:COM) was at $95 per barrel.

data.title: DTST projects about $2 million 2026 burn rate while pursuing AI-enabled acquisitions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Data Storage Corporation (DTST) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Charles Piluso framed FY2025 as transformational, saying “2025 was the most consequential year for Data Storage Corporation's 25-year history,” and tied the delayed filing to “additional time to complete our year-end audit,” driven by “the sale of our CloudFirst subsidiary, the classification and settlement of many of our outstanding warrants and the completion of a tender offer.”
  • CEO Piluso detailed three capital actions: “we monetized CloudFirst for a total transaction value of $40 million,” which “generated approximately $31.6 million in net proceeds and a $20.1 million gain”; “we returned $29.3 million of that capital directly to shareholders through a tender offer at $5.20 per share, reducing our outstanding share count by approximately 72%”; and “we entered 2026 debt-free with over $10 million in capital, a clean balance sheet.”
  • CEO Piluso cautioned that reported profitability was not recurring, stating, “we reported a net income of $19.2 million for the year compared to $500,000 for 2024,” but “this level of profitability reflects the CloudFirst transaction and other nonrecurring events.”
  • CFO Chris Panagiotakos said continuing operations now reflect “only our continuing operations, specifically our Nexxis subsidiary,” and reported, “Sales from continuing operations were $1.4 million for the year ended December 31, 2025, an increase of $164,000 or 13.4% compared to $1.2 million in the prior year.”
  • CEO Piluso positioned the company as an M&A vehicle, saying DTST is “a NASDAQ-listed acquisition platform with capital, flexibility and a clear mandate,” with targets including “AI-enabled vertical SaaS GPU infrastructure, cybersecurity and SOC-related services as well as scalable technology businesses with recurring revenue models.”

Outlook

  • Management did not provide revenue or EPS guidance in the prepared remarks; instead, CFO Panagiotakos set an operating cash-use expectation in Q&A: “the burn rate for 2026 will be probably about $2 million for the year. being a public company.”
  • CEO Piluso described cost actions and overhead expectations, stating, “as we move through 2026, we expect corporate overhead to decline meaningfully,” and reiterated the operational goal: “Our objective is to ensure that the earning power of this company is driven by operations, not onetime events.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing of post-CloudFirst uncertainty around liquidity, the company now discussed a completed tender outcome and a simplified base, with CEO Piluso stating, “we reset the company,” and “we expect to provide meaningful updates in the near term as these opportunities evolve.”

Financial Results

  • CFO Panagiotakos reported FY2025 continuing-operations performance and cost drivers, including “Selling, general and administrative expenses… increased… to $4.2 million,” driven by “a $507,000… increase in noncash stock-based compensation” tied to “accelerated vesting… in connection with the sale of the CloudFirst business.”
  • CFO Panagiotakos highlighted a year-end liquidity step-up: “We ended the quarter with cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of approximately $41 million at December 31, 2025, compared to $12.3 million at December 31, 2024.”
  • CEO Piluso emphasized the operating base is now Nexxis, stating, “Today, our core operating business is Nexxis and it's performing,” and cited business quality improvements, including “reducing customer concentration, with no single customer accounting for more than 10% of the revenue.”

Q&A

  • Matthew Galinko, Maxim Group: Asked whether AI/HPC valuations were “within reason” or “overheated”; CEO Charles Piluso responded, “the valuations are all over the place,” adding that he heard of “pre-revenue” efforts where “people are hoping to get $700 million,” while noting DTST is looking for “parts that you can play in… you're not going to get crushed.”
  • Matthew Galinko, Maxim Group: Asked whether cash-on-hand helps move counterparties; CEO Piluso said DTST has spoken to “21 companies,” described MSP valuations as “trading at 1x, but they're trying to get 2.5x revenue,” and outlined structures like separating “meat and potatoes” MSP cash flows from experimental software via “a joint venture… where we have the opportunity to buy it if you actually deploy it.”
  • Matthew Galinko, Maxim Group: Asked for run-rate/burn and Nexxis expectations; CFO Panagiotakos said, “the burn rate for 2026 will be probably about $2 million for the year,” while CEO Piluso said Nexxis is “profitable,” but DTST may spend “a little bit of money… to improve the SEO side of things,” while staying “very disciplined for the first acquisition.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts tone was neutral-to-skeptical, with valuation and capital deployment risk implied by questions like whether the AI market is “overheated,” and a practical focus on burn/run-rate and deal pacing.
  • Management tone was confident in strategy but cautious on frothy markets; CEO Piluso said “the valuations are all over the place” and characterized many AI conversations as aspirational, while still stating, “we expect to provide meaningful updates in the near term.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, management sounded less focused on tender uncertainty and more on selectivity, with the shift from Q3’s cash-position range discussion to Q4’s emphasis on a “clean balance sheet” and active pipeline language.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • In Q3, management emphasized the tender offer as pending and hard to forecast, including CEO Piluso’s range discussion (“between $5 million and $15 million”); in Q4, management described the tender as completed and highlighted the resulting share count reduction of “approximately 72%.”
  • Strategic focus in Q3 was “DSC 2.0” and “selective opportunities” that were “not current commitments”; in Q4, CEO Piluso described DTST as “a NASDAQ-listed acquisition platform” and said the company is “actively evaluating opportunities,” including having “identified and… pursuing a number of strategic opportunities with an emerging GPU infrastructure segment.”
  • Analyst focus shifted from tender mechanics and SG&A run-rate in Q3 to acquisition valuation realism and whether cash accelerates deal flow in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Piluso flagged earnings quality risk by stating FY2025 profitability “reflects the CloudFirst transaction and other nonrecurring events,” and said the company is “focused internally on improving efficiency” so future results are “driven by operations.”
  • Management highlighted valuation volatility and market froth in AI-related targets, with CEO Piluso noting “pre-revenue” valuation expectations and saying “the valuations are all over the place.”
  • CFO Panagiotakos pointed to cost pressures tied to public-company overhead and transaction-related compensation, while also stating, “We expect expenses to decrease… for… 2026… since a significant number of its employees are no longer working for us.”

Final Takeaway

Management presented FY2025 as a reset built around monetizing CloudFirst, executing a $5.20-per-share tender that reduced shares by about 72%, and repositioning DTST as a debt-free acquisition platform. Leadership emphasized that FY2025 net income was driven by nonrecurring events, while pointing to Nexxis as the continuing operating base and signaling that 2026 priorities are to lower overhead, manage an estimated ~$2 million annual burn as a public company, and pursue selective acquisitions across AI-enabled infrastructure, cybersecurity, and other recurring-revenue technology models.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Bank7 signals 4.40%-4.45% core NIM outlook as it targets moderate single-digit loan growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bank7 Corp. (BSVN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "we are happy with our results today" and "we are really proud of our ability to manage our NIM and to properly mix our balance sheet, and we're not concerned about rates going out or rates going up, we're positioned either way" (President, CEO & Vice Chairman Thomas Travis).
  • On growth expectations, management kept its annual lens intact: "our goals for the year remain intact. We're still thinking moderate single digit" while noting pace versus late 2025: "coming off of the third and fourth quarter we had last year where we had really robust growth... we're not at that pace" (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Jason Estes).
  • On energy exposure and demand sensitivity, management emphasized it is not a swing factor for the story: "it's at a 10-year low... it was 8 -- a little over 8% of the portfolio" and "I wouldn't expect the energy piece to be causing a lot of dynamic change" (President, CEO & Vice Chairman Travis).
  • "we are modeling in that same range, 4.40% to 4.45% from a core NIM perspective, and on the loan fee side of things, kind of reverting back to the normal of 28 to 35 basis points" (Executive VP & CFO Kelly Harris).

Outlook

  • "we are modeling in that same range, 4.40% to 4.45% from a core NIM perspective" and loan fees "reverting back to the normal of 28 to 35 basis points" (Executive VP & CFO Harris).
  • For Q2 operating cadence, management provided internal ranges: "For Q2 on the expense side, we're projecting internally in the range of $9 million to $9.2 -- and on the fee side, low end of $750,000, upwards of $850,000" (Executive VP & CFO Harris).
  • On the oil-and-gas-related gross-up, management framed it as temporary and not earnings-driving: "continued the expense offsetting the income. So not really material to the bottom line, but temporarily grossing up both sides of the P&L" (Executive VP & CFO Harris).

Financial Results

  • The prepared remarks did not state GAAP revenue, EPS, or net income figures in the transcript; management instead pointed listeners to "the major metrics in the deck" (President, CEO & Vice Chairman Travis).
  • Margin drivers called out in Q&A included deposit-side progress: "We did make some really good progress on the liability side, cost of funds, and that was related to our talented bankers continuing to bring in some quality core deposits" (Executive VP & CFO Harris).
  • Management quantified quarter items affecting recurring margin discussion: "from a core NIM perspective, I think the nonaccrual interest net up was $1.1 million" and "on a fee perspective, it's closer to 1.7" (Executive VP & CFO Harris).

Q&A

  • Adam Kroll, Piper Sandler: asked whether loan growth expectations changed after payoffs pressured period-end balances; Executive VP Estes replied, "our goals for the year remain intact. We're still thinking moderate single digit" and added, "We expect pretty sizable payoffs" in Q2 that would be offset by bookings.
  • Adam Kroll, Piper Sandler: asked about NIM ex-fees with stable rates; Executive VP Harris answered, "we are modeling... 4.40% to 4.45%" core NIM and loan fees "28 to 35 basis points."
  • Adam Kroll, Piper Sandler: asked about capital build, M&A, and tolerance for higher capital; President, CEO & Vice Chairman Travis said, "we ended the quarter at 15.96% on risk base" and "we've always been active in the M&A space" for "the right strategic opportunities."
  • Will Jones, KBW: asked if deposit competition could push deposit costs higher later in the year; President, CEO & Vice Chairman Travis said, "I don't see a massive fluctuation or any meaningful fluctuation in deposit costs" assuming no rate increase.
  • Will Jones, KBW: pressed on provisioning/reserves sustainability; Executive VP Estes said, "our credit book is as clean as it's ever been" and outlined sensitivity: "if we grow the book... yes, we'll have to provision a little bit more" while slower growth could reduce the need.
  • Will Jones, KBW: asked again on buybacks; President, CEO & Vice Chairman Travis said, "share buybacks really don't add franchise value" and framed any future use as conditional: "driven by a good share repurchase price and no other alternatives."
  • Jordan Ghent, Stephens: asked for downgrade detail; Executive VP Estes said, "we had a large builder developer relationship... and that was the one Tom referenced that we think will pay off this week."
  • Jordan Ghent, Stephens: revisited M&A approach including MOE; President, CEO & Vice Chairman Travis said, "I think the answer is both" and "we've not deviated from our thinking on that."
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: asked about oil-and-gas fee/expense impacts; Executive VP Harris reiterated gross-up mechanics, and President, CEO & Vice Chairman Travis added, "we have accomplished our goal" and expects it "either gone altogether or diminished quite a bit over the next few months."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-slightly skeptical, with repeated probing on sustainability (deposit costs, normalized margin, provision outlook, and capital returns), including "what is the messaging on the provision and reserve levels going forward?" (Will Jones, KBW).
  • Management’s tone was confident in positioning and execution, emphasizing steadiness: "we're positioned either way" on rates (President, CEO & Vice Chairman Travis) and "it's steady as she goes for Bank7" in closing.
  • Versus last quarter’s emphasis on rate cuts and margin downside scenarios, management’s language shifted toward stability and less rate-path sensitivity, with "we don't expect anything materially different" on margin (President, CEO & Vice Chairman Travis).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Rate narrative shifted: in Q4 2025 management discussed "a couple of rate cuts" and potential NIM pressure with more cuts, while in Q1 2026 management noted markets "were expecting rate cuts" but now "maybe the rates will go the other way" and asserted, "we're positioned either way" (President, CEO & Vice Chairman Travis).
  • Loan growth messaging moved from 2025’s "really robust growth" backdrop to a moderated pace while keeping targets: "still thinking moderate single digit" and "we're not at that pace" (Executive VP Estes).
  • Capital return posture stayed consistent: Q1 2026 reiterated buybacks as non-core—"don't add franchise value"—while highlighting ongoing M&A intent and a high capital position (President, CEO & Vice Chairman Travis).

Risks and Concerns

  • Macro uncertainty was repeatedly flagged as the swing factor for rates and credit: "the increase in commodity prices associated with the Middle Eastern conflict" (President, CEO & Vice Chairman Travis) and "we really don't know what the economy is going to do for the rest of the year" (Executive VP Estes).
  • Credit risk discussion centered on monitoring and specific resolution prospects: "we have quoted a payoff for this Friday" tied to a material remaining NPA, and if it closes, "the net effect would be NPAs of somewhere in that $4 million to $5 million range" (President, CEO & Vice Chairman Travis).

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized balance-sheet positioning for a shifting rate outlook, maintained expectations for “moderate single digit” loan growth despite expected payoffs, and guided to a “4.40% to 4.45%” core NIM range with loan fees normalizing to “28 to 35 basis points.” Capital remained high at “15.96% on risk base,” with continued M&A interest and a consistent stance that buybacks “don't add franchise value,” while oil-and-gas-related income/expense was described as a temporary gross-up expected to be “gone altogether or diminished quite a bit over the next few months.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Canadian Copper secures C$96M project financing from OR Royalties, Ocean Partners
data.description:
Canadian Copper (CNDIF) up 15.6% in Tuesday's trading after saying it secured as much as C$96M in committed capital from OR Royalties (OR) and Ocean Partners U.K. to develop its 100%-owned Murray Brook project and Caribou process plant—together called the Bathurst complex—in Canada's New Brunswick province.

OR Royalties' (OR) share of the funding will total C$38M in exchange for a 20% life-of-mine payable silver and gold stream from Bathurst, Ocean Partners—an existing 17% shareholder in Canadian Copper (CNDIF) - will provide up to C$48M in project debt in exchange for 100% of Bathurst's offtake rights, and the companies will provide up to C$10.5M in equity subscriptions.

OR Royalties' (OR) will provide Canadian Copper (CNDIF) with C$28M in cash proceeds for the precious metals stream.

Canadian Copper's (CNDIF) preliminary economic assessment from July 2025 outlined a 13.2-year mine plan for the Murray Brook deposit that proposed an open pit with four development phases at an average production rate of 3,300 metric tons/day of mineralized material at a strip ratio of 5.0:1 for 13.2 years.

data.title: TransDigm shares rise after upbeat preliminary results signal strong quarter
data.description:
Shares of TransDigm Group (TDG) rose as much as 5.6% Tuesday after the aerospace supplier pre-announced stronger-than-expected fiscal second-quarter results, prompting a positive reaction from analysts.

In a research note, Matt Akers of BNP Paribas said the early figures point to “solid” underlying performance, reinforcing expectations for continued growth across the company’s key segments.

Preliminary numbers

TransDigm (TDG) said it expects revenue of about $2.54 billion to $2.545 billion for the quarter, ahead of both BNP Paribas estimates of $2.445 billion and broader Wall Street expectations near $2.417 billion.

The company also forecast earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization of roughly $1.33 billion to $1.335 billion, exceeding both the firm’s estimate and consensus projections.

Akers noted that the stronger profitability, combined with share repurchase activity during the quarter, could translate into higher earnings per share than previously modeled.

Capital activity in focus

The preliminary results were disclosed alongside a proposed $1.25 billion debt transaction tied to an acquisition and roughly $800 million in share buybacks completed during the quarter.

The analyst said those buybacks were not previously incorporated into forecasts, suggesting potential upside to earnings estimates once full results are released.

Growth signals across segments

While the company didn't provide detailed commentary on business trends, Akers said the performance likely reflects steady demand across its end markets.

BNP Paribas had been projecting about 10% overall growth for the quarter, including similar gains in aftermarket sales, stronger growth in original equipment and mid-single-digit expansion in defense.

The update may also bode well for other aerospace suppliers, particularly those with exposure to the aftermarket, which has been supported by high aircraft utilization and maintenance demand.

Outlook

BNP Paribas maintained its Outperform rating on TransDigm (TDG), with a price target of $1,700, implying significant upside from recent levels.

Akers said the firm will revisit its forecasts when the company provides full earnings details in the coming weeks, but added that the early read suggests no signs of a slowdown in the business.

data.title: Google's DeepMind launches more advanced Gemini robotics
data.description:
Google's (GOOG) (GOOGL) DeepMind AI research lab introduced its recently upgraded Gemini robotics model and is making the system available to developers via the Gemini API and Google AI Studio.

Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6 is the latest iteration of Google’s (GOOG) (GOOGL) reasoning-first model that can understand their environments with “unprecedented precision" over predecessors ER 1.5 and Gemini 3.0 Flash. 

“By enhancing spatial reasoning and multi-view understanding, we are bringing a new level of autonomy to the next generation of physical agents,” Google said in a statement.

Through these advancements, Google’s (GOOG) (GOOGL) Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6 specializes in precise object detection, spatial and physical reasoning capabilities, motion detection, and success detection that allows a robot to choose between retrying a failed attempt or progressing to the next stage of a task.

Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6 can also achieve highly accurate instrument readings by using agentic vision. This combines visual reasoning with code execution by first zooming into an image, and then using pointing and code execution to estimate proportions and intervals to get a precise reading.

To build an even more robust model, Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) invites the robotics community to provide specific mode failures of the Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6 “to ensure Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6 provides maximum value.”
